cli-e2e-tests is the only zone that exercises cli.js, web.js, and ci.js — but those entry points delegate to spawned subprocesses that must have been built beforehand; the suite has no pre-test build guard, so a stale dist/ silently tests an older version of the code

Low cohesion (0.29) and high coupling (0.71) suggest the crash detector and recovery hook are tightly coupled to their callers in web-dashboard rather than forming a self-contained module — consider whether these two files should be absorbed into the web-dashboard zone.

The bidirectional import relationship with web-dashboard (3 imports each way) creates a zone-level cycle; use-crash-recovery.ts likely depends on viewer state, while the detector is consumed by the viewer — a one-directional dependency would be cleaner.
Only crash-detector.ts has a test; use-crash-recovery.ts is untested despite being the hook consumed by the viewer.
Cohesion of 0.29 is below the warning threshold — the two files in this zone (hook and detector) are more coupled to web-dashboard than to each other, suggesting a zone boundary mismatch.
Coupling of 0.71 exceeds the warning threshold; the crash recovery subsystem has high external dependency, which reduces its reusability and increases change risk.
use-crash-recovery.ts lacks a unit test; given that crash recovery is a reliability-critical code path, this gap should be addressed.

Call graph shows 0 outgoing calls despite 3 cross-zone import edges to web-dashboard — the imports are type-only (interfaces, enums) rather than runtime function calls. This means the zone has no runtime coupling to web-dashboard, only compile-time coupling, which is significantly less risky than the import-graph coupling score implies.

crash-recovery's 0 outgoing call-graph edges vs 3 outbound import edges to web-dashboard indicates the coupling is type-import-only. The zone's effective runtime isolation is much higher than its cohesion/coupling scores suggest — the metric overstates the structural risk.

crash-recovery has only 1 incoming call-graph edge despite 3 cross-zone import edges from web-viewer. The zone is consumed by a single caller at runtime, making it a de facto singleton utility. This strengthens the case for absorbing it into web-dashboard as an internal sub-module rather than maintaining a separate zone boundary.

crash-detector.ts installs a `beforeunload` event listener as a module-level side effect at runtime (not in a function call), meaning the listener is registered the moment the module is first imported. This side effect is invisible to callers and complicates hot-reload, SSR, and test isolation — `resetCrashDetector()` exists to compensate but the coupling is implicit.

crash-detector.ts exports a `_testHelpers` object containing private constants and internal functions via the module's public API surface (line 299). This leaks implementation details into the production bundle and creates an undocumented testing contract that consumers must know to avoid.

Both crash-detector.ts and tick-timer.ts independently implement the same module-level singleton pattern (mutable module vars + a `reset*()` escape hatch for tests). The convergent design is undocumented — a third such module added without awareness of this convention may omit the reset function, breaking test isolation.

`_testHelpers` is exported through the production module surface of crash-detector.ts, bundling internal implementation details (storage keys, private functions) into the public API. Test-only exports should use a separate test-support file or conditional barrel to avoid polluting the production interface.

Zone name 'crash-recovery' is derived from the consumer hook (use-crash-recovery.ts) rather than the core logic module (crash-detector.ts). The zone is named after its application-layer consumer, not its infrastructure implementation — contrary to the typical convention of naming a zone after its primary artifact.

Rename the zone to 'crash-detection' to reflect its foundational implementation (crash-detector.ts) rather than its hook consumer (use-crash-recovery.ts), which would then be absorbed into web-viewer where it is consumed. Zone names derived from consumer hooks rather than implementation modules invert the normal naming convention.

Hench is the largest single zone at 160 files with perfect cohesion — the gateway pattern is successfully containing cross-package coupling.
The agent/analysis sub-system (adaptive, review, spin) is a logical cluster within the zone; watch for it growing into a candidate for its own sub-module.

The rex-gateway module is the sole coupling surface for a 160-file zone — any breaking change to the 8 re-exported functions has a blast radius spanning the entire hench package simultaneously, making gateway API stability a higher-risk concern than for smaller consumers.

A single gateway file (src/prd/rex-gateway.ts) is the only cross-zone coupling surface for 160 files; gateway API breakage has maximum blast radius within hench — no incremental migration path exists if the rex API changes.

The rex-gateway module (src/prd/rex-gateway.ts) is the sole coupling surface for 160 files, but there is no sub-zone boundary between the agent loop code and the analytical sub-system (agent/analysis/). If the analytical sub-system grows its own cross-package imports, they will route through the same single gateway file, making it impossible to audit which part of hench is the actual consumer of each re-export.

Hench has no internal interface layer between its agent orchestration and its rex-gateway consumer code — the gateway is imported directly by whatever file needs it, meaning gateway API changes require tracking usages scattered across 160 files with no intermediate abstraction to constrain the blast radius.

The rex-gateway is imported directly by scattered consumer files across 160 files with no internal hench interface layer. When the rex gateway API changes, every call site must be updated individually with no intermediate abstraction to narrow the scope of change. An internal adapter or facade within hench that wraps the gateway would contain the blast radius.

packages/hench/package-lock.json is anomalous in a pnpm workspace — pnpm manages dependencies through a single root lockfile (pnpm-lock.yaml) and per-package lock files should not exist. A stale or divergent package-lock.json in hench could cause npm-vs-pnpm dependency resolution conflicts if a developer accidentally runs npm install inside the package directory.

PNG binary assets (Hench.png, Hench-F.png) are stored directly in the hench package root with no documented convention for binary asset management — their presence in the package bundle is unintentional unless explicitly excluded in .npmignore or package.json 'files' field.

packages/hench/package-lock.json should not exist in a pnpm workspace. It can cause dependency resolution conflicts if npm is run inside the package. Verify it is not committed to source control or add it to .gitignore, and document that pnpm is the sole package manager for this monorepo.

Hench.png and Hench-F.png in the hench package root have no documented role in the package contract. If they are README assets only, reference them via absolute URL or move to a docs/assets/ directory and add a 'files' field to package.json to exclude them from published bundles. The web package has the same pattern (SourceVision.png, SourceVision-F.png) — a single monorepo-level asset convention would cover both.

Import edges count type imports as coupling, inflating the coupling score for zones that only share interface types with their neighbors. Flagging zones as high-coupling based solely on import edges produces false positives; call-graph outgoing edge counts are the more actionable signal.

Three test/script zones all have coupling=0 and cohesion=1, confirming a clean separation between infrastructure and production code; this pattern should be documented as an explicit architectural invariant and enforced in CI

Gateway enforcement logic is spread across two zones using two different mechanisms (Vitest test runner in cli-e2e-tests, standalone .mjs scripts in viewer-gateway-tests); a single canonical location and mechanism would eliminate the risk of silent divergence

The orchestration tier (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) is covered only by the cli-e2e-tests E2E zone and has no unit-level coverage; a single breaking change in subprocess argument handling could pass all unit tests and only fail at E2E, slowing detection

The call graph reveals a concrete domain-calls-CLI layering violation within rex: packages-rex:unit-analyze makes 239 calls into packages-rex:cli, the inverse of the expected CLI→domain direction. This means the domain layer has a runtime dependency on CLI implementation details, not just a circular import — a change to CLI output formatting or argument parsing could break domain logic.

The web-server zone (5 files, cohesion 0.75) is too small to maintain an independent zone identity and receives 3 imports from web-viewer. It has all the characteristics of a micro-cluster that should be documented as a sub-module of web-viewer rather than a peer zone, since it has no consumers other than web-viewer.

The crash zone (3 files, cohesion 0.29) has the lowest cohesion of any zone in the codebase. At 3 files with cohesion 0.29, at least one of those files is nearly unrelated to the others by the algorithm's measure. This warrants a file-level audit to determine if a file is misclassified rather than assuming the zone represents a coherent error-boundary sub-system.

tick-timer has 27 incoming call-graph edges, making it the highest-fan-in primitive in the web package. It is also implied to be a small zone (by contrast with web-dashboard at 354 files). Small high-fan-in zones are the highest-risk zones for breaking changes: a single API rename in tick-timer propagates to all 27 call sites simultaneously with no intermediate abstraction to absorb the change.

The packages-rex:unit-analyze → packages-rex:cli circular sub-zone dependency (239 + 100 calls) should be resolved by extracting a shared internal module (e.g., rex/src/shared/) that both the CLI and the analyze domain can import from — eliminating the need for either direction to import from the other's layer.

Gateway enforcement is split across two zones using two different test mechanisms (Vitest in cli-e2e-tests, standalone .mjs scripts in viewer-gateway-tests). These two enforcement paths have no shared contract definition, so a new cross-package import pattern could satisfy one check while evading the other — creating a false sense of complete coverage.

The convergent singleton pattern across crash-detector.ts and tick-timer.ts (module-level mutable state, zero framework dependencies, reset() for testing) is an undocumented architectural convention. Codifying it as an explicit 'browser singleton module' pattern in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md would prevent future implementations from omitting the test-reset contract.

analyze-panel.ts defines its own Proposal type hierarchy locally instead of sharing types with the server-side API layer, creating a silent schema drift risk. The prd-analysis-panels zone has no gateway import from rex or web-dashboard for PRD types, meaning the client-side type representation of PRD proposals is entirely detached from the canonical rex schema.

The `_testHelpers` export pattern used in crash-detector.ts (exporting private internals under a namespaced object for test access) is the only instance of this pattern in the codebase. It conflicts with the project's gateway convention of keeping public module surfaces clean — a documented decision on how to handle test-only exports (separate barrel, vitest `__mocks__`, or accepted exception) would prevent inconsistent adoption.

The three zero-coupling zones (viewer-gateway-tests, dev-analysis-scripts, cli-e2e-tests) collectively form a 'verification tier' — the layer responsible for validating architectural correctness — but there is no single command, Makefile target, or CI step that runs all three together. A contributor enforcing a gateway change must know to run e2e tests, the standalone .mjs scripts, AND the analysis scripts independently.

Gateway enforcement is implemented using three different mechanisms across the verification tier: Vitest test runner (architecture-policy.test.js in cli-e2e-tests), standalone .mjs scripts (check-gateway-regex.mjs, check-gateway-test.mjs in viewer-gateway-tests), and no mechanism for dev-analysis-scripts. This fragmentation means a single gateway violation could be caught by one mechanism but missed by another if they diverge in their regex or rule definitions.

The physical test root split (monorepo tests/ vs packages/*/tests/) is load-bearing for zone assignment — files in the wrong root end up in the wrong zone algorithmically. Without a documented convention enforced in CI, new test files placed in the wrong root will be silently misclassified into unrelated zones, as happened with the web viewer unit tests now grouped with monorepo gateway scripts.

No zone in this batch has any incoming call-graph edges, confirming the verification tier is correctly terminal — nothing in production imports from tests or scripts. However, the absence of outgoing call-graph edges from dev-analysis-scripts also means the scripts are not being invoked from any orchestrated pipeline; they are purely manual tools with no automated trigger.

packages-rex:unit-analyze calls packages-rex:cli 239 times — domain code calling into the CLI layer inverts the expected CLI→domain dependency direction. This is a concrete layering violation (not just a circular import): CLI argument parsing and output formatting are leaking into domain semantics. Extracting a shared internal module that both layers can import would eliminate the cycle without violating the layering contract.

The crash zone (3 files, cohesion 0.29) has the lowest cohesion score in the codebase. At least one file in this zone is algorithmically near-unrelated to the others. A file-level audit is needed to determine if a misclassified file should be pinned to web-viewer or another zone before treating crash-recovery as a coherent architectural unit.

tick-timer is a small primitive zone with 27 incoming call-graph edges — the widest blast radius of any zone in the web package. It has no abstraction layer between itself and its 27 dependents. A breaking API change in tick-timer propagates simultaneously to all consumers with no intermediate facade to narrow the impact scope.

Gateway enforcement logic is split across two mechanisms (Vitest runner in cli-e2e-tests, standalone .mjs in viewer-gateway-tests) with no shared rule definition. If the regex patterns or gateway file lists diverge, one mechanism will pass while the other fails, producing inconsistent enforcement depending on which check runs. A single authoritative gateway rule source (e.g., a shared config file) consumed by both mechanisms would prevent silent divergence.

The verification tier (viewer-gateway-tests, dev-analysis-scripts, cli-e2e-tests) has no orchestrating command that runs all architectural checks in sequence. A developer validating a structural change must discover and run three separate mechanisms manually; the gap means partial validation is the norm rather than the exception.

Test files are distributed across two physical roots (monorepo tests/ and packages/*/tests/) with no documented convention for which root to use. The algorithmic zone detector groups files by import proximity, not physical intent, so misplaced test files silently land in wrong zones. New contributors have no signal about where to add new test files.

Binary assets (PNG files) appear in both the hench-agent zone (Hench.png, Hench-F.png) and web-package-scaffold zone (SourceVision.png, SourceVision-F.png) with no monorepo-wide convention for where package assets live, whether they are excluded from npm publish, or whether they should be referenced via URL rather than bundled. A single assets/ convention documented in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md would cover both packages.

Zone IDs use inconsistent compound conventions: some use plain names ('crash', 'panel', 'tests', 'scripts', 'e2e') while others use hyphenated compound names ('web-viewer', 'web-server', 'web-unit', 'hench-agent'). The plain-name zones are all either very small or infrastructure-only, but the naming rule distinguishing when to use a compound name vs a plain name is undocumented — future zones added by sourcevision's community detection will follow neither convention consistently.

The gateway naming convention differs in path structure between hench (src/prd/rex-gateway.ts — path reflects the consumer sub-domain) and web (src/server/rex-gateway.ts, src/server/domain-gateway.ts — path reflects the server layer). PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md documents the gateway pattern's rules but not the canonical file placement convention, leaving future gateway authors to infer the correct path from examples.

The '-F.png' suffix on Hench-F.png and SourceVision-F.png across both packages implies a 'filled' or 'flat' icon variant, but the suffix convention is not documented anywhere. A reader encountering these files for the first time cannot determine the distinction between the plain and -F variants without prior context.

The three satellite zones (crash-recovery, tick-timer, prd-analysis-panels) all import into web-viewer with zero direct imports between each other. This strict fan-out topology — satellite → hub, never satellite → satellite — is an implicit no-direct-peer-import invariant that is currently unenforced and undocumented. A future contributor importing crash-recovery utilities from prd-analysis-panels would silently violate it.

elapsed-time-memoization.test.ts spans two production zones (tick-timer and web-dashboard/elapsed-time.ts) in a single test file, making it the only cross-zone test in the codebase. Its zone classification (under tick-timer by import proximity) means ElapsedTime component coverage is attributed to the wrong zone in any zone-level test coverage report.

The packages-rex:unit-analyze → packages-rex:cli intra-package circular dependency (239+100 calls) is the only 'must fix' structural issue in the codebase and is unguarded by any zone in the verification tier — gateway scripts cover only cross-package boundaries, cli-e2e-tests covers only orchestration behavior, and dev-analysis-scripts covers only hench. A targeted architecture-policy assertion for intra-package layer direction is missing.

All three verification-tier zones (viewer-gateway-tests, dev-analysis-scripts, cli-e2e-tests) validate different architectural layers (cross-package boundaries, output artifact freshness, orchestration behavior) but share no common rule registry. A contributor adding a new gateway file must independently update check-gateway-regex.mjs, check-gateway-test.mjs, and potentially architecture-policy.test.js with no single source of truth to keep them aligned.

The verification tier's self-validation gap creates a meta-risk: the mechanisms that enforce architecture are themselves unverified. check-gateway-*.mjs regex patterns, test-zone-consistency.mjs zone ID assertions, and architecture-policy.test.js structural checks are all trust-on-first-write with no regression protection against the checks themselves being silently broken by a future refactor.

Add a binary asset management convention to PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md covering: (1) where package logos/icons should live relative to the package root, (2) whether they must be excluded from npm publish via 'files' or .npmignore, and (3) whether the '-F' suffix for icon variants is an official convention. Both hench and web currently bundle PNG assets without documented intent.

PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md documents what gateways must do (re-export only, one per upstream package) but not where they must live. Hench places its gateway at src/prd/rex-gateway.ts; web places gateways at src/server/{rex,domain}-gateway.ts. Add a 'Gateway file placement' rule specifying the canonical directory and naming pattern to prevent future gateways from being created in ad-hoc locations.

Zone ID naming is inconsistent across the codebase: small/infrastructure zones use plain names (crash, panel, tests, scripts, e2e) while larger or web-specific zones use hyphenated compounds (web-viewer, hench-agent, web-server). Document a zone naming convention in sourcevision configuration or PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md so that auto-detected zones and manually pinned zones follow a predictable scheme.

Document the no-direct-peer-import invariant for web satellite zones (crash-recovery, tick-timer, prd-analysis-panels must not import from each other — only from web-viewer). Add a gateway policy rule enforcing this in the existing gateway check scripts. Currently the constraint exists empirically but has no enforcement mechanism, making it invisible to contributors adding new cross-zone imports.

Cross-zone test files (files that import from two or more production zones) should be placed in a dedicated tests/cross-zone/ or tests/integration/ directory rather than either zone's unit test folder. elapsed-time-memoization.test.ts is currently misclassified into tick-timer by the zone algorithm because its closest import is tick-timer.ts, despite also covering web-dashboard's elapsed-time.ts. A documented convention for cross-zone tests would prevent future misclassification.

Create a single gateway-rules.json (or equivalent) at the monorepo root that both check-gateway-regex.mjs and architecture-policy.test.js consume as the authoritative source of gateway file paths and allowed import patterns — eliminates silent divergence between two enforcement mechanisms

Add an intra-package layer-direction check to architecture-policy.test.js that parses import paths within each package and asserts domain files do not import from cli/ subdirectories — directly guards the existing packages-rex circular sub-zone violation and prevents recurrence

Add a pnpm verify script (or Makefile target) that runs all three verification-tier mechanisms in sequence: vitest e2e, check-gateway-regex.mjs, check-gateway-test.mjs, and test-zone-consistency.mjs — removes the current requirement for contributors to manually discover and run four separate commands

This strengthens the case for absorbing it into web-dashboard as an internal sub-module rather than maintaining a separate zone boundary.
Generated artifact HENCH_CALLGRAPH_FINDINGS.md is committed but has no CI regeneration-and-diff guard; it can silently go stale after hench source changes

A single gateway file (src/prd/rex-gateway.ts) is the only cross-zone coupling surface for 160 files; gateway API breakage has maximum blast radius within hench — no incremental migration path exists if the rex API changes.

Orchestration-to-domain boundary is enforced at runtime only (subprocess spawning); a mismatched CLI argument or removed subcommand will produce a silent runtime failure with no compile-time safety net.

prd-analysis-panels is a strict terminal node in the call graph (0 incoming, 3 outgoing). The bidirectional import relationship with web-dashboard noted in the zone-level cross-imports does not produce bidirectional call-graph edges — the reverse direction carries only type imports. No circular runtime dependency exists.

tick-timer's 27:1 incoming-to-outgoing call ratio makes it the highest fan-in utility in the web package. Any change to its interface propagates to 27 call sites — it warrants a stable, explicitly versioned API contract and should never import from its consumers.

usage-analytics-service has the cleanest architecture profile of any zone in this batch: one gateway, one consumer, zero transitive outbound calls. It is a textbook example of the gateway pattern applied correctly at zone granularity.
Zone conflates web-package unit tests with monorepo-root contract scripts; splitting into separate zones aligned to their physical location would improve discoverability and ownership clarity

At a 6504:55 internal-to-outgoing call ratio, web-dashboard's outbound import edges are almost entirely type-only. Runtime coupling to the rest of the monorepo is effectively zero, which validates the gateway pattern at the call level, not just the import level.

web-viewer is the de facto internal API surface for the web-dashboard zone cluster: crash, panel, and web-unit all route through web-viewer rather than importing directly from web-dashboard. This hub-within-hub topology is architecturally sound but undocumented.

Zone coupling metric (0.33) is an artifact of viewer UI files co-classified with build infrastructure; it does not reflect true architectural coupling in the build layer and should not be acted on until zone pinning is applied.
Architectural rules documented here are enforced by tests in two separate zones with no automated link between them; the documentation and its enforcement tests can diverge without detection.

crash-recovery's 0 outgoing call-graph edges vs 3 outbound import edges to web-dashboard indicates the coupling is type-import-only. The zone's effective runtime isolation is much higher than its cohesion/coupling scores suggest — the metric overstates the structural risk.

tick-timer has zero coupling to web-dashboard at the call level (1 outgoing edge, none to web-dashboard) while web-dashboard has 4 inbound import edges from it — the dependency flows strictly upward from primitive to consumer, confirming a clean unidirectional layering that the zone-level bidirectional label obscures.

E2E tests that spawn CLI subprocesses share no documented workspace isolation strategy; if tests run concurrently and both write to .rex/prd.json or .sourcevision/, results are non-deterministic. No test uses a dedicated temp directory per test file.

`_testHelpers` is exported through the production module surface of crash-detector.ts, bundling internal implementation details (storage keys, private functions) into the public API. Test-only exports should use a separate test-support file or conditional barrel to avoid polluting the production interface.

hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs produces HENCH_CALLGRAPH_FINDINGS.md but has no fail-fast guard that detects missing or stale input artifacts; silent success on a cold checkout produces a misleading (empty or stale) report with no error signal.

The rex-gateway is imported directly by scattered consumer files across 160 files with no internal hench interface layer. When the rex gateway API changes, every call site must be updated individually with no intermediate abstraction to narrow the scope of change. An internal adapter or facade within hench that wraps the gateway would contain the blast radius.

The orchestration tier's architectural boundary (no direct package imports) is enforced solely by developer convention — no ESLint rule, TypeScript path mapping, or CI check prevents a direct `import` from cli.js into a domain package. A single accidental import would collapse the tier silently.

claude-integration.js is a service file at the monorepo root that bypasses the gateway pattern entirely. Any cross-package imports it makes are invisible to gateway audits and import-graph coupling scores, creating an unmonitored coupling surface outside all four tiers of the dependency hierarchy.

Proposal, ProposalFeature, and ProposalTask types are defined locally in analyze-panel.ts rather than imported from a shared rex schema or gateway. The local copy will silently diverge from the API response shape if the server-side PRD proposal format changes.

`useState(() => { loadPending(); })` in analyze-panel.ts misuses the useState initializer to trigger an async network call. This is not the intended use of the initializer (which sets initial synchronous state) and bypasses the standard effect lifecycle, making cleanup and double-invocation behavior undefined.

Web viewer unit tests (graph-interaction.test.ts, graph-zoom.test.ts) are excluded from packages/web per-package test coverage because they reside outside the package boundary in this zone; per-package coverage reports for the web package silently undercount viewer UI test coverage.

Zone contains files from two physically distinct roots (packages/web/tests/unit/viewer/ and tests/) with unrelated purposes; zone name 'viewer-gateway-tests' implies viewer scope only, actively misleading contributors about the gateway scripts' monorepo-wide scope. Should be split into two zones aligned to physical location and concern.

Post-pinning, web-package-scaffold will shrink to ~4 build-infrastructure files. Without an explicit zone pin or documented rationale, the next sourcevision analyze run may dissolve this zone and re-classify its files into an adjacent zone, undoing the corrective pinning work.

Standardize to one convention and add a docs/README.md index linking each file with a one-line description to make the directory navigable without reading all filenames.

The fix for the useState misuse in analyze-panel.ts requires two coordinated edits: (1) add useEffect to the import on line 9: `import { useState, useCallback, useEffect } from 'preact/hooks'`, and (2) replace line 74 with `useEffect(() => { loadPending(); }, [])`. Both changes must land together — changing line 74 without updating the import will fail the TypeScript build.

The current scheme sorts rotated files before the active log in lexicographic order, making chronological navigation non-obvious. Document the chosen convention explicitly or align with the POSIX suffix-numbering standard.

Zone "Unit" (packages-rex:unit) has critical risk (score: 0.67, cohesion: 0.33, coupling: 0.67) — justified: Large cross-cutting zone spanning analyze/ and recommend/ subsystems (19 files).
